turns.content: I'm building a quick prototype to test latency, and I've already written code that reads from one stream while writing to another using try_clone. It works fine but I'm wondering if there's a better way to handle the shutdown logic or avoid some overhead.

You're right to look into this because cloning a TcpStream is expensive since it involves copying internal buffers and state. Instead of calling try_clone, consider using separate TcpListeners for read and write paths if you control the connection setup, or manage two distinct TcpStream handles from one socket with careful synchronization.
That sounds reasonable but I thought cloning just created a shallow copy that shared ownership so it should be cheap. Is there actually overhead in the clone operation itself?

You're making an assumption about try_clone being shallow when it's not; it deep copies the stream state including any pending data or buffer pointers, which means significant performance cost. That's why you should avoid cloning unless absolutely necessary and instead manage two separate TcpStream instances from the original socket.
I see now that I was wrong about how cloning works. But what about shutdown? Does calling shutdown(Write) on one side affect reading from the other?

Yes, calling shutdown(Write) on a TcpStream will prevent any further writes and may cause the read end to receive an EOF immediately if both ends are managed by the same process. You must call shutdown(Both) only when you're done with the entire connection or use shutdown(Read) selectively if your protocol allows it.
Got it, so I need to be careful about which shutdown variant I pick. Is there a way to disable delays while reading and writing simultaneously?

Yes, you can call set_nodelay(true) on each TcpStream handle before starting your read/write operations to avoid TCP delay queuing overhead. However, note that set_nodelay affects the socket level and doesn't guarantee zero latency at application layer since framing protocols still add processing time.
That makes sense. So for my prototype I should avoid try_clone entirely and instead use two separate TcpStream instances with set_nodelay enabled?

Exactly, that's the right approach for your performance goals. You'll want to bind a listener once, accept connections to get one TcpStream, then derive read and write handles from it without cloning, ensuring both sides operate efficiently with nodelay enabled.
Perfect, I think I understand the distinction now. One last question though: if I'm using a framing protocol over raw TCP, will shutdown(Both) cause data loss during active reads?

Calling shutdown(Both) while actively reading can interrupt pending operations and potentially lose in-flight data unless you explicitly flush or drain the buffer before shutting down. Always ensure all writes are complete and any buffered reads are processed before invoking shutdown(Both).
